experiments.purpose: Improvement-discovery experiment for the __unchecked: init situation in Tagged + Carrier. Tests six variants (V1 baseline, V2 generic-throws with default closure, V3 V1+V2 overloaded, V4 parallel protocol with generic-throws requirement, V5 default extension on real Carrier, V6 protocol requirement + default impl) plus V7 commentary on validation-mandatory types. Discriminates hypotheses about whether a public init pattern can subsume per-domain custom-init declarations and how throwing validation should be admitted.
experiments.statusDetail: H1 / H3 / H4 / H5 / H6 CONFIRMED; H2 REFUTED. The simple 'single API via E == Never inference from default empty closure' shape doesn't compile. Working answers: V5 (default extension on Carrier) gives every Carrier conformer a throwing init for free; V6 (requirement + default impl) adds polymorphism. Structural trade-off (V7): both V5 and V6 add non-opt-out-able public surface; validation-mandatory types lose the protection the current __unchecked:-only design provides. Decision deferred to research-process between (a) keep current __unchecked: discipline, (b) ship V5/V6 accepting the structural softening, (c) introduce a marker protocol for validation-mandatory types.

experiments.purpose: Empirical verification of three niche/edge-case absence rationales. (A) CustomStringConvertible covers the playground-display use case (String(describing:) and tagged.description both produce '42'). (B) Codable conditional handles Tagged-as-value via Swift's default synthesis — empirically discovered to produce a KEYED container ({"rawValue": N}), symmetric encode/decode, round-trip works. (C) Decode failure on malformed JSON produces a single informative DecodingError (typeMismatch / dataCorrupted / keyNotFound) — no double-try fallback means no error masking.
experiments.statusDetail: All three niche absences classified HARD. Surfaced an empirical detail: Swift's synthesized Codable on a struct with one stored property produces a keyed container, not single-value — recorded in research doc. Round-trip is symmetric.

experiments.purpose: Empirical verification of the Foundation-independence axiom for Tagged Primitives + consumer-side opt-in pattern for LocalizedError and UUID convenience inits. (a) Foundation-free proof: experiment imports Tagged_Primitives WITHOUT import Foundation, builds and runs cleanly — confirms [PRIM-FOUND-001] axiom. (b) Demonstrates that LocalizedError and UUID conformances would require Foundation, which is forbidden at the primitives layer. (c) Consumer-side pattern documented: in a Foundation-importing consumer's package, the conformance is one-line and lives at the consumer's layer.
experiments.statusDetail: Foundation-dependent conformances classified HARD-BY-AXIOM. [PRIM-FOUND-001] structurally forbids Foundation in primitives; SLI inherits the axiom. Consumer-side opt-in is the canonical pattern.

experiments.purpose: Empirical verification of ExpressibleByArrayLiteral / ExpressibleByDictionaryLiteral safe-Swift authorability + per-domain alternative. (a) Safe-Swift array-literal conformance compiles for per-element-type specialization (RawValue == [Int]) — no unsafeBitCast needed. (b) General parametric conformance (RawValue: ExpressibleByArrayLiteral) would require unsafeBitCast as pointfree does. (c) Per-domain wrapper structs (UserGroup, LookupTable) handle literal construction cleanly via variadic-to-Array/Dict conversion at the wrapper level — no bitcast needed.
experiments.statusDetail: Array/Dict literal classified HARD-BY-POLICY. SLI excluded due to strict-memory-safety stance + per-element specialization narrowness. Per-domain wrapper is the canonical alternative.

experiments.purpose: Empirical verification that @dynamicMemberLookup cannot be retroactively added to Tagged + demonstrates explicit-unwrap and consumer-wrapper alternatives. (a) Absence proof: tagged.name does not compile (Tagged has no such member). (b) Retroactive-attribute test (reject-test-no-passthrough.swift.txt): @dynamicMemberLookup on extension fails — must be on type declaration. (c) Explicit unwrap pattern: tagged.rawValue.name marks every type-boundary crossing. (d) Consumer wrapper pattern: UserWrapper has own @dynamicMemberLookup forwarding to Tagged-stored UserRecord — provides passthrough ergonomics for domains that explicitly want it.
experiments.statusDetail: Classified HARD absence. @dynamicMemberLookup not retroactively applicable; SLI cannot opt in. Recommended pattern is explicit .rawValue access or consumer-side @dynamicMemberLookup wrapper.

experiments.purpose: Empirical verification of Tagged: AdditiveArithmetic authorability + the domain-blind arithmetic footgun + per-domain alternative. (a) Option B (SLI-style opt-in) compiles on Swift 6.3.1 — function-style witnesses (static func +/-, static var zero) bypass ~Escapable blocker. (b) Domain-blind footgun demonstrated: UserID + UserID compiles even though adding user IDs is semantically meaningless; processUsers(start:count:) returns a UserID with no domain meaning. (c) Compounding with literals: userA + literal(5) compiles, reducing to Int arithmetic on what was supposed to be a phantom-typed wrapper. (d) Per-domain Counter: domains with meaningful arithmetic author the conformance directly; the operation IS the semantic.
experiments.statusDetail: Family classified SOFT structurally / HARD semantically. Recommendation against SLI inclusion despite structural authorability — package-level granularity too coarse for the operator-forwarding footgun. Per-domain conformance (Cardinal, Counter, etc.) is the correct alternative.

experiments.purpose: Empirical verification of Tagged: Sequence and Tagged: Collection authorability, wrapper-vs-content conflation cost, and explicit-unwrap alternative. (a) Both Option B opt-in conformances compile on Swift 6.3.1 — function-style witnesses bypass ~Escapable blocker. (b) Generic algorithm (sumElements over T: Sequence) treats Tagged<Tag, [Int]> identically to [Int] — phantom Tag invisible to the algorithm. (c) Explicit .rawValue unwrap preserves wrapper-boundary visibility at every iteration / first / count call site.
experiments.statusDetail: Sequence and Collection both classified SOFT. Confirms function-style witnesses authorable; wrapper-vs-content conflation real but consumer-accepted on opt-in. Default-safe pattern is .rawValue.first / .rawValue.forEach / etc. — preserved at the main-target level by absence.

experiments.purpose: Empirical verification of Tagged: LosslessStringConvertible authorability + per-domain alternative. (a) Option B (SLI-style opt-in) compiles on Swift 6.3.1 — init?(_:) is function-style; description witness is satisfied by the existing CustomStringConvertible conformance. (b) Within-domain roundtrip works: UserTag.ID("99") parses correctly. (c) Lossy-from-Tagged-perspective demonstrated: string '99' parses as both UserTag.ID(99) and OrderTag.ID(99); receiver type determines the Tag. (d) Per-domain wrapper UserIDWrapper preserves within-domain lossless guarantee.
experiments.statusDetail: LosslessStringConvertible classified SOFT. Confirms function-style init? + computed-forward description witness combination is authorable; per-domain wrapper alternative also works.

experiments.purpose: Empirical verification of Tagged: Identifiable authorability + per-domain alternative. (a) Option B (SLI-style opt-in) compiles on Swift 6.3.1 — Identifiable's getter is computed-forward (var id: ID { rawValue.id }), not direct stored-property access; bypasses the ~Escapable blocker. (b) Identity-inversion cost demonstrated: UserTag.ID and OrderTag.ID with the same DomainKey are observed as id=42 by Identifiable consumers — phantom Tag is invisible to the protocol layer. (c) Per-domain alternative (Option C) demonstrated: User: Identifiable with id: UserTag.ID preserves phantom-typed identity; User.ID and Order.ID are distinct types at the protocol layer.
experiments.statusDetail: Identifiable classified SOFT absence. Confirms computed-forward witnesses authorable (similar bypass mechanism as CustomStringConvertible's description witness). Per-domain pattern (Tagged as Identifiable.ID, not Tagged: Identifiable) is the recommended alternative.

experiments.purpose: Empirical verification of Tagged: Strideable authorability + per-domain alternative. (a) Option B (SLI opt-in) compiles on Swift 6.3.1 — function-style requirements (distance(to:), advanced(by:)) bypass the structural ~Escapable blocker. (b) Same-domain range iteration via the opt-in conformance works as expected ([1,2,3,4,5] for userA...userB). (c) Cross-domain ranges rejected by phantom Tag (compile error). (d) Per-domain alternative demonstrated via SparseUserID where stride increments by valid-ID-position (not raw-Int diff), illustrating domain-correct stride semantics independent of the SLI path.
experiments.statusDetail: Strideable classified SOFT absence (SLI-eligible). Confirms function-style witnesses authorable; per-domain alternative also authorable. Caveat: SLI Strideable reactivates the literal-conformance footgun.

experiments.purpose: Empirical verification of the principled absence of Tagged: RawRepresentable. (a) Reject test demonstrates three constraint shapes all fail with 'the get accessor cannot return a ~Escapable result' (captured in reject-test-conformance.swift.txt). (b) Acceptance test demonstrates the consumer alternative — a domain-specific wrapper struct (UserID) that owns its own RawRepresentable conformance, performs meaningful failable-init validation (non-negative IDs), forwards storage to Tagged, and works with stdlib RawRepresentable-constrained APIs.
experiments.statusDetail: Confirms the absence is STRUCTURAL (not preference): RawRepresentable is not ~Escapable-aware, so Tagged's structural ~Escapable declaration propagates through synthesized rawValue getter witness regardless of constraint shape. Reclassified RawRepresentable from SOFT to HARD absence; consumer wrapper is the canonical alternative.

experiments.purpose: Verify Tagged can support ~Copyable RawValue
Verify Tagged produces identical codegen to raw values at -O
Verify UInt-backed Tagged types reject negative literals at compile time
Verify two constrained extensions on Tagged can each provide typealias Char
experiments.statusDetail: Result (REFUTED) remains historically accurate, but the Viewable protocol the experiment targets was deleted in commit 9ac9b04 (2026-04-23) when Tagged migrated to Ownership.Borrow.`Protocol`. No current code path depends on this finding.
experiments.supersededBy: tagged-primitives/9ac9b04 — Viewable deleted; Tagged conforms to Ownership.Borrow.`Protocol` instead

experiments.purpose: Viewable protocol with ~Copyable ~Escapable associated type for Tagged.View typealias
experiments.statusDetail: Result (CONFIRMED) remains historically accurate, but the Viewable protocol the experiment prototypes was deleted in commit 9ac9b04 (2026-04-23) when Tagged migrated to Ownership.Borrow.`Protocol`. No current code path depends on this finding.
experiments.supersededBy: tagged-primitives/9ac9b04 — Viewable deleted; Tagged conforms to Ownership.Borrow.`Protocol` instead

experiments.purpose: Single nested Tagged.View struct with ~Copyable, ~Escapable; stored properties cannot be conditional per RawValue
experiments.statusDetail: Result (CONFIRMED) remains historically accurate, but Tagged no longer ships a nested View struct. Commit 9ac9b04 (2026-04-23) migrated to Ownership.Borrow.`Protocol` with Borrowed associated type resolution. No current code path depends on this finding.
experiments.supersededBy: tagged-primitives/9ac9b04 — Viewable deleted; Tagged conforms to Ownership.Borrow.`Protocol` instead

experiments.purpose: Revalidate the Bit.Index byte-to-bit overload resolution footgun on Swift 6.3.1; determine whether Strideable absence (current) vs presence (future) changes the outcome
experiments.statusDetail: Footgun STRUCTURALLY DORMANT without Strideable on Tagged; ACTIVE with Strideable. Current production safety is accidental, not principled. Also includes sub-package production-reality-check/ that confirms the dormant state against real Bit.Index + test support.

experiments.purpose: Gate Tagged's ExpressibleByIntegerLiteral/FloatLiteral on a RawValue marker protocol (TaggedLiteralSafe) — opt-in for Int/UInt32/Double etc., opt-out for Ordinal/Cardinal — and verify footgun prevention even with Strideable
experiments.statusDetail: Main mechanism CONFIRMED (marker gating prevents footgun). Per-tag opt-in (adding ExpressibleByIntegerLiteral for a specific Tag == X, RawValue == Y combination alongside the gated blanket) is REFUTED — Swift rejects as 'conflicting conformance ... there cannot be more than one conformance, even with different conditional bounds'.

experiments.purpose: Test whether consumer packages can each independently add ExpressibleByIntegerLiteral conformance to Tagged for their specific Tag/RawValue combinations without cross-module conflict
experiments.statusDetail: Single consumer works; two consumers with disjoint constraints fail at the client site with 'type alias X requires the types A and B be equivalent'. Swift's one-conformance-per-(type, protocol) rule applies across module boundaries. Exactly one package in the whole build graph can own Tagged's literal conformance.

experiments.purpose: Revalidate the 'Swift 6.3 closure-parameter-lifetime gap for ~Escapable types' claim documented in Tagged.swift. Hypothesis: `package mutating func modify(_:)` with inout ~Escapable RawValue closure parameter cannot compile on Swift 6.3.
experiments.statusDetail: FIXED on Swift 6.3.1 (Xcode 26.4.1). Minimal Variant 2 compiles and runs. Production Tagged.swift widened to RawValue: ~Copyable & ~Escapable; 59/59 tagged-primitives tests continue to pass.

experiments.purpose: Validate that a `.root` accessor descending to the bottom-most Underlying can be added without re-introducing cascade in Tagged's Carrier conformance. Tests four hypotheses across the design space: H1 (extension-only depth-1), H1c (stacked-constraint extensions for deeper levels), H2 (generic dispatch via where-clauses, found depth-coupled), H3 (recursive Root via associatedtype, requires conditional Tagged conformance — re-introduces Property.View blocker), H4 (split BaseCarrier + Rooted protocols, preserves unconditional Tagged conformance AND supports generic dispatch at any depth via Rooted.Root cascade).
experiments.statusDetail: All four hypotheses validated. Recommended design: H4 (split BaseCarrier + Rooted). Achieves Carrier-based universal unifier without per-domain `*.`Protocol`` types, with no cascade in Tagged, while preserving the Property.View case (Tagged<Tag, Ownership.Inout>: BaseCarrier even though Inout is not Rooted). Generic dispatch via `where C: Rooted, C.Root == UInt` matches bare Cardinal AND Tagged<Tag, Cardinal> AND nested Tagged at any depth.
